1: wayyaḥel haˁam liznot (וַיָּחֶל הָעָם לִזְנוֹת) — 'the people began to commit fornication.' Ḥalal in hiphil = to begin, but also to profane/defile. The form carries both: to begin is to defile. Liznot (from zanah = to fornicate, play the harlot) is the vocabulary of spiritual and physical unfaithfulness throughout the prophets (Ezek 16, 23; Hos 1-3). Shittim (שִׁטִּים, acacia trees) — Israel's final campsite before Canaan; the first failure happens at the threshold of the promise.
2: wayyiqreˀu laˁam leziḇḥey ˀeloheyhen — the Moabite women invited Israel to the sacrifices of their gods. Cultic meals bind worshippers to the deity they honor: eating at a god's table creates allegiance. Paul uses this principle in 1 Cor 10:18-21: those who eat at the table of demons share with demons. The Baal Peor meal is the anti-type of the Lord's Supper — communion with what is not God.
3: wayyiṣamed Yisraˀel levaˁal Peˁor (וַיִּצָּמֶד יִשְׂרָאֵל לְבַעַל פְּעוֹר) — Israel yoked itself to Baal Peor. Ṣamed = to yoke, attach (as animals in a team). The metaphor is deliberate: Israel entered a working partnership with a foreign god — a yoking that is the opposite of the exclusive covenant. Paul cites this incident directly: 'We must not indulge in sexual immorality as some of them did, and twenty-three thousand fell in a single day' (1 Cor 10:8).
4: hôqaˁ ˀotam lYHWH (הוֹקַע אוֹתָם לַיהוָה) — 'hang/expose them to the LORD.' Yaqaˁ = to expose, impale, dislocate; a public execution that removes the curse from the community. The public hanging before YHWH in the sun is covenantal procedure: the community dissociates itself from the guilty. Liyhwh — the execution is done before/for YHWH, not merely as punishment but as religious repair.
5: ṣimdu levaˁal Peˁor (צְמוּדִים לְבַעַל פְּעוֹר) — yoked to Baal Peor. The hiphil of the previous verse's niphal: the leaders are to put to death those who yoked themselves. The double use of ṣamed reinforces the yoking metaphor: the covenant break is described with agricultural language — they have put on the wrong yoke. Jesus offers his own easy yoke (Matt 11:29-30) in contrast to yoking to false gods.
6: An Israelite man brings a Midianite woman leˀeyney Mosheh weleˀeyney kol-ˁedat (לְעֵינֵי מֹשֶׁה וּלְעֵינֵי כָּל-עֲדַת) — before the eyes of Moses and the whole congregation. The brazen public defiance makes the act a theological provocation, not merely a personal sin. The people were weeping at the tent entrance (boke = crying in mourning, perhaps already aware of the plague); the man acts in their sight while they weep.
7: Pinḥas ben-Elˁazar ben-Aharon hakohen — Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron. The full genealogy establishes priestly credentials: third-generation Aaronide. wayiqom miqerev haˁedah (וַיָּקָם מִקֶּרֶב הָעֵדָה) — he rose from the midst of the congregation. His is an individual act against communal paralysis; the whole congregation was weeping while one man acted.
8: wayyidqor ˀet-sheneyhem (וַיִּדְקֹר אֶת-שְׁנֵיהֶם) — 'he pierced/stabbed through both of them.' Daqar = to pierce, run through with a weapon. The same verb appears in Zech 12:10 ('they will look on me, the one they have pierced') — the word for the eschatological mourning over the one who was pierced is the same as Phinehas' act. Through Phinehas the plague stopped (v.8b); through the pierced one the divine wrath is ultimately exhausted.
9: wayyihyu hammetim bammagepha (וַיִּהְיוּ הַמֵּתִים בַּמַּגֵּפָה) — 'those who died in the plague were twenty-four thousand.' This is the largest single plague death toll in the wilderness narrative. Paul in 1 Cor 10:8 gives the number as twenty-three thousand (in one day) — a near-parallel; the difference may reflect counting conventions (per day vs. total) or a different textual tradition.
10: YHWH speaks to Moses. The divine speech introduces the theological interpretation of Phinehas' act — his action precedes God's verbal affirmation. In the narrative, physical action stops the plague; God's word follows to explain, reward, and consecrate what zeal accomplished. The sequence matters: zeal acted first; verbal covenant followed.
11: qinˀ ˀet-qinˀati (קִנֵּא אֶת-קִנְאָתִי) — 'he was zealous with my zeal/jealousy.' A figura etymologica: he was jealous with my jealousy. Qinˀah = jealousy, zeal — both the passionate desire for exclusive allegiance and the burning wrath when that allegiance is violated. YHWH calls Israel's exclusive devotion with the vocabulary of a jealous spouse (Exod 20:5, ˀel qannaˀ = jealous God). Phinehas' zeal is a human participation in divine jealousy — the covenant's passion expressed through a covenant member.
12: brit shalom (בְּרִית שָׁלוֹם) — covenant of peace. The same phrase used for the eschatological covenant in Isa 54:10 ('my covenant of peace will not be removed') and Ezek 34:25 ('I will make a covenant of peace with them'). Phinehas receives the covenant vocabulary that belongs to the messianic age. His zeal that stopped the plague is a type of the atonement that makes peace — shalom as the result of wrath absorbed.
13: brit kehunnat ˁolam (בְּרִית כְּהֻנַּת עוֹלָם) — covenant of everlasting priesthood. The Phinehas covenant guarantees his priestly line. kiper ˁal-bney Yisraˀel (כִּפֶּר עַל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) — he made atonement for Israel. The atonement vocabulary (kipper, ˁalah) applies to Phinehas' action: his spear-thrust was an act of atonement. This is unusual: kipper typically involves blood rituals at the altar. Here the priestly-zealot's direct action stops divine wrath and accomplishes what the altar rites accomplish.
14: The Israelite man is identified: Zimri ben-Salu nˀsiˀ bet-ˀav laShimˁoni — Zimri son of Salu, a leader of Simeon. The Simeonite detail resonates: Simeon as a tribe was severely reduced after Baal Peor (ch 26 records only 22,200 Simeonites vs. 59,300 in the first census). The leader of the tribe that suffered most from the plague is the man who provoked the plague at its climax.
15: The Midianite woman: Kozbi bat-Tsur (כָּזְבִּי בַת-צוּר) — Cozbi daughter of Zur. Kozbi from kazav = to lie, deceive; Cozbi = 'my lie' or 'deceiver.' The woman whose name means deception is the vector of the Baal Peor apostasy. Zur is a Midianite leader — her status makes the public act more provocative: this is not a casual encounter but an alliance-through-marriage attempt.
16: YHWH's speech to Moses: tsaror ˀet-hamMidyanim (צָרוֹר אֶת-הַמִּדְיָנִים) — 'treat the Midianites as enemies.' The root tsarar = to bind, constrain, distress; the niphal means 'enemy' (the one who constrains you). The Midianites used a religious and sexual strategy (through Balaam's counsel, see 31:16) where a military strategy would have failed. Their treachery through enticement rather than war is the model of spiritual deception throughout Scripture.
17: nakeh ˀot-haMidyanim (נַכֵּה אֶת-הַמִּדְיָנִים) — 'strike the Midianites.' The command comes immediately after the theological explanation: because they acted deceptively (niklˀu lakem). The judgment is proportionate to the method: they attacked through intimacy; they will be answered with force. The Midianite war (Num 31) executes this command.
18: bˀnikhleyhem (בְּנִכְלֵיהֶם, in/through their schemes/treachery) — from nakal = to scheme, act treacherously. The Midianites planned what Balaam could not accomplish through cursing (Num 22-24). The Balaam-Peor connection: Balaam advised Midian to use women as the weapon that curses couldn't provide. The treachery was theological: they used Israel's own sexuality as the mechanism of apostasy.
